QF.id: XIII Introduction to the Underlying Markets_9

question.text: Which type of yield curve indicates that short-term interest rates are higher than long-term rates?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** An inverted yield curve occurs when short-term yields exceed long-term yields. This can happen when policy rates are kept high to control inflation or during severe asset-liability mismatches. It is often considered a predictor of economic recession. **The Trap:** A normal yield curve is upward sloping (long > short). A flat curve means similar rates across maturities. A humped curve rises then falls. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series IV Ch1 covers yield curve shapes extensively. Series I references interest rate parity which relates to yield curves across countries. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** An inverted yield curve historically precedes recessions — derivatives traders use this signal to adjust hedging strategies across asset classes.
